cards.q: Define bronchiolitis.
cards.a: An inflammatory obstruction of the small airways — the bronchioles and surrounding tissues; an acute, predominantly viral infection of the lower respiratory tract.

cards.q: Is bronchiolitis viral or bacterial?
cards.a: Predominantly viral, with few cases attributed to other organisms.

cards.q: When does bronchiolitis peak seasonally?
cards.a: During the rainy season in tropical countries (winter in temperate countries).

cards.q: In which age group does bronchiolitis occur, and what is the peak age?
cards.a: The first 2 years of life; peak age 2-8 months (average 6 months).

cards.q: What is the sex distribution of bronchiolitis?
cards.a: Commoner in males, M:F 2:1.

cards.q: How long do most cases of bronchiolitis last?
cards.a: Most are mild, lasting 7-10 days.

cards.q: What is the mortality of hospitalized bronchiolitis and who is most affected?
cards.a: About 1-2%, occurring most in children under 6 months.

cards.q: List the predisposing factors for bronchiolitis.
cards.a: Rainy season; cold dry dusty harmattan; not being breastfed; overcrowding and day-care; cigarette smoke exposure; low birth weight; chronic lung disease; congenital heart lesions; and older family members as a source of infection.

cards.q: What is the commonest cause of bronchiolitis?
cards.a: Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV).

cards.q: List the viral causes of bronchiolitis.
cards.a: RSV (commonest), adenovirus, parainfluenza 1/2/3, human metapneumovirus, bocavirus, rhinovirus and influenza virus.

cards.q: Which adenovirus types cause severe bronchiolitis, and what complication?
cards.a: Types 7, 11 and 21; more likely to be complicated by bronchiolitis obliterans.

cards.q: Which RSV group is more severe?
cards.a: RSV group A (more severe than group B).

cards.q: How is the pathogen for bronchiolitis acquired?
cards.a: By inhalation of infected respiratory droplets, or inoculation of the nostrils/conjunctivae from contaminated hands of contacts.

cards.q: Where does the initial viral infection and replication occur?
cards.a: In the infant's upper respiratory tract, before spreading down.

cards.q: What is the dominant host-related factor for severe bronchiolitis?
cards.a: Age-related peripheral airway dimension — the innately smaller peripheral airways of infants predispose to severe obstruction.

cards.q: Why does the lack of collateral ventilation channels worsen bronchiolitis in infants?
cards.a: Infants have few pores of Kohn and canals of Lambert, so a blocked bronchiole cannot be ventilated around, leading to atelectasis.

cards.q: What other two host factors predispose infants to bronchiolitis?
cards.a: A greater number of mucus-secreting goblet cells, and genetic age-related airway hyper-reactivity to RSV and parainfluenza type 3.

cards.q: What immune response does RSV incite?
cards.a: Eosinophil degranulation with release of eosinophil cationic protein (cytotoxic to epithelium), increased Toll-like receptor-4 expression, and increased cytokines/chemokines, IgE and inflammatory mediators.

cards.q: List the pathological abnormalities in bronchiolitis.
cards.a: Necrosis of the respiratory epithelium, destruction of ciliated cells, intraluminal bronchiolar plugs of sloughed debris and fibrin, and peribronchial mononuclear infiltration.

cards.q: Which pathological features are more prominent in adenovirus than RSV?
cards.a: Extensive epithelial destruction, pulmonary parenchymal necrosis and hyaline membrane formation.

cards.q: When does recovery of the airway epithelium and cilia occur?
cards.a: Epithelial regeneration begins ~4 days after the acute phase; ciliary activity is restored in ~2 weeks or more.

cards.q: What three changes reduce the bronchiolar lumen in bronchiolitis?
cards.a: Oedema of the mucosa, accumulation of mucus and cellular debris, and constriction of the bronchiolar wall muscles.

cards.q: Why does airway resistance rise so steeply in infant bronchiolitis?
cards.a: Resistance rises exponentially (four-fold) as airway radius decreases, so small reductions in tiny infant airways cause large increases in resistance and work of breathing.

cards.q: What is ball-valve obstruction and its result?
cards.a: Because the airway is narrower during expiration, air enters but cannot leave, causing early air trapping and progressive hyperinflation.

cards.q: What is check-valve obstruction and its result?
cards.a: Complete obstruction, so trapped distal air is resorbed and the child develops patchy atelectasis.

cards.q: Why does hypoxaemia occur early in bronchiolitis?
cards.a: Because of ventilation-perfusion mismatch — blocked areas receive blood but not air.

cards.q: Why does hypercapnia develop late in bronchiolitis?
cards.a: With severe obstruction and tiring of respiratory effort, less air reaches the alveoli to remove CO2, so arterial PCO2 rises.

cards.q: List the symptoms of bronchiolitis.
cards.a: Sneezing and clear rhinorrhoea, diminished appetite, fever 38.5-39°C or subnormal temperature, respiratory distress, paroxysmal wheezy cough, dyspnoea, irritability, tachypnoea and apnoea.

cards.q: List the signs of bronchiolitis.
cards.a: Markedly increased work of breathing, nasal flaring and retractions, fine inspiratory crepitations, rhonchi, prolonged expiratory phase, reduced breath sounds, and hepatomegaly/splenomegaly.

cards.q: Why are the liver and spleen palpable in bronchiolitis?
cards.a: Due to lung hyperinflation pushing the diaphragm down — it is displacement, not true organomegaly.

cards.q: Why is the expiratory phase prolonged in bronchiolitis?
cards.a: Because of small-airway obstruction that makes it hard to push air out (ball-valve effect).

cards.q: List the complications and co-morbidities of bronchiolitis.
cards.a: Dehydration; apnoea and bradycardia (especially under 2 months); acute otitis media; hypoxaemia with or without hypercarbia; viral pneumonia; secondary bacterial pneumonia; recurrent wheezing or asthma; and urinary tract infection.

cards.q: Which complications are especially associated with infants under 2 months?
cards.a: Apnoea and bradycardia (with features of dehydration).

cards.q: What long-term respiratory association does bronchiolitis have?
cards.a: Recurrent wheezing or asthma.

cards.q: What chest X-ray findings occur in bronchiolitis?
cards.a: May be normal; peribronchial thickening; hyperinflation with splayed ribs, flattened diaphragms and relative microcardia; patchy atelectasis; increased bronchovascular markings; interstitial streaks from the hilum; and increased AP diameter on the lateral film.

cards.q: What is the FBC usually like in bronchiolitis?
cards.a: The total WBC count and differentials are usually normal (consistent with a viral illness).

cards.q: What is the role of pulse oximetry in bronchiolitis?
cards.a: To detect hypoxaemia and guide the need for supplemental oxygen — the key practical investigation.

cards.q: When is viral testing used in bronchiolitis?
cards.a: When the diagnosis is uncertain or for epidemiological purposes (immunofluorescence, PCR or culture).

cards.q: List the differential diagnoses of bronchiolitis.
cards.a: Bronchial asthma, wheeze-associated respiratory infection (WARI), congestive cardiac failure, pertussis, aspirated foreign body, gastro-oesophageal reflux disease, croup, retropharyngeal abscess, acute epiglottitis, and impacted upper-airway foreign body.

cards.q: How do you distinguish bronchiolitis from asthma?
cards.a: Bronchiolitis is typically a first wheeze in an infant under 2 in the rainy season; asthma is recurrent, episodic and bronchodilator-responsive with atopy.

cards.q: What is the mainstay of bronchiolitis treatment?
cards.a: Supportive care — there is no cure; you support the infant while the virus resolves.

cards.q: List the supportive treatments for bronchiolitis.
cards.a: Oxygen therapy, increased fluids and feeds, a trial of bronchodilators, and corticosteroids/anti-inflammatory agents only if benefit outweighs risk.

cards.q: Which single treatment is most important in bronchiolitis?
cards.a: Oxygen therapy (for the hypoxaemia).

cards.q: When is ribavirin used in bronchiolitis and how is it delivered?
cards.a: In severe cases, immunocompromised hosts or infants with cyanotic CHD; delivered by a small-particle aerosol generator (SPAG) over 3-5 days, 18-20 hours/day.

cards.q: When are antibiotics indicated in bronchiolitis?
cards.a: Only when there is concomitant severe bacterial infection — not routinely.

cards.q: Is physiotherapy routine in bronchiolitis?
cards.a: No — it may help mobilise secretions but is not routinely done.

cards.q: Outline the prevention and control of bronchiolitis.
cards.a: Palivizumab immunoprophylaxis (IM) for high-risk infants; health education on hand decontamination; encouraging breastfeeding; minimizing spread in creches/nurseries; and discouraging cigarette and domestic smoke exposure.

cards.q: What is palivizumab and how is it given?
cards.a: A monoclonal antibody used for immunoprophylaxis in high-risk infants, given intramuscularly.

cards.q: What is the biggest practical preventive measure against bronchiolitis spread?
cards.a: Hand decontamination (hand-washing), since it spreads on contaminated hands.

cards.q: OSPE — A chest X-ray of a wheezy infant shows splayed ribs, flat diaphragms and a small-looking heart. What is this and the diagnosis?
cards.a: Hyperinflation (with relative microcardia) — consistent with bronchiolitis.

cards.q: CLINICAL — A 6-month-old in the rainy season has coryza progressing to a wheezy cough, tachypnoea, nasal flaring and SpO2 89%. Diagnosis and immediate management?
cards.a: Acute bronchiolitis; admit, give oxygen with pulse oximetry, maintain fluids/feeds, trial of bronchodilators, and monitor for apnoea.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Why should you not routinely give antibiotics in bronchiolitis?
cards.a: Because it is predominantly viral (RSV), so antibiotics are reserved for concomitant bacterial infection.

cards.q: CLINICAL — A 6-week-old with bronchiolitis has episodes of stopping breathing. What complication is this and why is age relevant?
cards.a: Apnoea (with possible bradycardia) — a danger complication especially in infants under 2 months, who may deteriorate suddenly.

cards.q: CLINICAL — A wheezy hyperinflated infant has a liver palpable 3 cm below the costal margin. Is this hepatomegaly?
cards.a: No — the hyperinflated lungs push the diaphragm and liver down; it is displacement, not true hepatomegaly.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Which infants qualify for palivizumab prophylaxis?
cards.a: High-risk infants, e.g. those with chronic lung disease, congenital heart lesions or prematurity.
